entry.title: Transaction handling in CAP within try-catch and a loop
entry.content:
How does CAP handle DB errors that happen inside of try-catch clauses inside of a loop?

Below is my sample code. Assume only order #2 fails when updating "Users" and catch engages.

What will be saved?

What I observed in our real application (when we got a deadlock due to concurrent update to "Users"): book "b2" and user "u2" wouldn't be updated and catch was engaged. But then order "2" would be updated (obviously because it happens after the first loop). As if each try-catch would have its own transaction scope. It looked like all other objects (from order 1 and order 3) were updated too.

When I tested in local environment with SQLite -> the catch did not engage, everything ran without exceptions but then the whole request would fail with 500 and nothing would be saved. As if it remembers that the whole request is poisoned and fails in the very end. I assume handling is different when using HANA. I must mention that locally I simulated error differently (by trying to set the ID to an already used value). I am not sure how to simulate concurrent writes from multiple instances in a local environment...

 

this.on('test', async (req) => {

  const goodOrders = [];
  const badOrders = [];
  for (const order of req.data.value) {
    try {
      await cds.update('Books', order.bookID).with({ stock: { '+=': -1 } });
      goodOrders.push(order);
      // Assume "deadlock exception" for user "u2" from order "2".
      await cds.update('Users', order.userID).with({ totalBooks: { '+=': 1 } });
    } catch (error) {
      badOrders.push(order);
    }
  }

  for (const goodOrder of goodOrders) {
    await cds.update('Orders', goodOrder).with({ state: 'OK' });
  }

  return {
    goodOrders,
    badOrders,
  }
})

POST /examples:

{
  "value": [
    { "ID": "1", "bookID": "b1", "userID": "u1" },
    { "ID": "2", "bookID": "b2", "userID": "u2-error!!!" },
    { "ID": "3", "bookID": "b3", "userID": "u3"}
  ]
}

 I would appreciate any links to documentation. The CAP doc is quite sparse around transaction handling...

entry.title: Part 1–Business process Credit Risk-Based Impairment in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
During a recent SAP S/4HANA project implementation, I worked on Credit Risk Based Impairment in compliance with IFRS9, While the Concept is Expected credit loss(ECL) from accounting perspective. Understanding how SAP handles ECL Posting , delta calculation and Write off customer balance.

This blog series shares end-to-end business process based on that implementation experience . 

  • First Part, business concept and process flow using a practical example. 
  • Second Part, will cover the configuration Steps.

 IFRS-9: Companies must record Provisions and expense related to Expected credit loss for Financial Asset and Accounts receivables before the actual loss occurs due to customer insolvency. The main Goal of ECL is to provide a realistic and predictive view of financial risk by on the estimating losses in advance. this helps companies to improve the financial transparency comply with international financial standard.

Expected credit loss ECL=Probability of default (POD) x Loss Given Default(LGD) x Exposure at default(EAD)

The business process in SAP

SAP Business Process Flow

As shown in the process flow, SAP S4/HANA applies a full business Credit risk based impairment cycle in compliance with IFRS9

  1. Identify the risk class in the customer master data (Low, medium, High).
  2. The AR Department records customer open invoices, and it's updated in both the Customer account and general ledger reconciliation account.
  3. The Finance team starts to calculate the ECL based on the risk class, POD% and outstanding customer open item (the calculation based on open item level) ECL=outstanding amount * POD%.

SAP automatically Posts the ECL expense account (P&L Account) and the Provision is posed as a negative amount in the main general ledger reconciliation account (Balance sheet account).

Loss Allowance = Probability of Default (PD) * Open Amount

Probability of Default are defined based on:

  1. Aging increments of the open items
  2. Risk Class of the business Partner

Mapping aging increments to risk classes and their corresponding Probability of Default (PD%).(Part 2 Configuration Step) Mapping aging increment to risk classes and corresponding Probability of Default (PD%)

Define Aging Increments for Advanced Valuation. (Part 2 Configuration Step)Aging Increments

The longer outstanding amount remains open, the higher customer’s risk class becomes, which increases the level of Probability of default, This results in an increase of provision calculation and impact on P&L statement.

Key Features

You only have to set up profit and loss (P&L) accounts. You don't need any reconciliation accounts for your balance sheet accounts. Postings are always made to the account to be valuated. Postings are made directly to the reconciliation accounts.

Post Credit-Risk-Based Impairment integrates the data of the Write Off Receivables app. When you've used the Write Off Receivables app, to write off receivables, you carry out Post Credit-Risk-Based Impairment as the next step. Credit-risk-based impairment recognizes and corrects the write-off postings and thus avoids wrong P&L and balance sheet results.

That means that you always have to carry out Post Credit-Risk-Based Impairment after you've done the last write-off in the Write Off Receivables app.

The business process starts with the following steps :

A) Master Data Setup – Risk Class Assignment
Based on our implementation experience, careful validation of customer risk classifications is essential before running the impairment process. Since the Probability of Default is derived from the assigned risk class, data quality in the business partner master record plays an important role in achieving accurate ECL results. So, The business process starts with identifying the risk class in customer master data.

In app-maintained business partner, under the General tab ”Credit Profile”, The risk class (Low, Medium, High).

BP Master Data

B) Customer Invoice Posting (Open Item Creation)

Customer outstanding open amount by 1 Million SAR on 01.04.2026, which creates an open item in Accounts Receivable and is also reflected in the General Ledger reconciliation account.

Customer Open Item

C) ECL Calculation – Post Credit-Risk-Based Impairment Run

One interesting behavior we observed during testing was that SAP calculates the impairment at the open-item level rather than at the customer balance level. This provides more accurate results, especially when customers have invoices with different aging periods. So in our example during executed via Schedule General ledger Jobs:

On 01.07.2026, Which is the valuation key date. Since the invoice is overdue by 90 days, the system applies the configured risk rules, for the risk class D (High Default Risk), a probability of default (POD)% of 60% is applied.

Resulting in an Expected Credit loss (ECL) of 600,000 (1,000,000 x 60%). the system posts the entry as:

Dr ECL Expense 600,000
             Cr Reconciliation Account 600,000

In the Schedule General Ledger Jobs app, select Post Credit-Risk-Based Impairment template to initiate the impairment calculation process.

Schedule General Ledger Jobs APP

Enter the required parameter for  Company Code 1000 and Leading Ledger 0L, with a valuation key date of 01.07.2026 and select Update run. Based on these parameters, SAP analyzes the outstanding receivables and determines the applicable impairment amount.scheduled General Ledger jobs

SAP calculates an Expected Credit Loss (ECL) of 600,000 SAR, based on an outstanding receivable of 1,000,000 SAR and a Probability of Default (PD) of 60%.scheduled General Ledger jobs

Customer Reconciliation GL Account is credited by 600,000 SAR, reducing the outstanding receivable balance after the impairment calculation posted.Receivables Recon GL Account

Note :
A common misunderstanding that  caused some confusion was the way SAP handles subsequent impairment runs. Rather than reposting the full impairment amount each time, the system only posts the additional amount needed to reach the new expected credit loss value. This delta approach helps prevent duplicate postings and keeps the financial results accurate. 

On the 31.10.2026 The outstanding customer amount has not paid, it’s overdue by 120 days and less than 1 year. At this stage, the risk increases so the Probability of Default (POD) rises from 60% to 80%.  As a result, the total expected credit loss (ECL) becomes 800,000 SAR. since the system already recognized 600,000 SAP in the previous stage run. The system will post only additional (delta) of 200,000 SAR as extra provision in the current period.

In the Schedule General Ledger Jobs app, select the Post Credit-Risk-Based Impairment template and enter the required parameter for  Company Code 1000 and Leading Ledger 0L, with a valuation key date of 31.10.2026 and select Update run. Based on these parameters, SAP analyzes the outstanding receivables and determines the applicable impairment amount.

scheduled General Ledger jobs

SAP calculates an Expected Credit Loss (ECL)  becomes 800,000 SAR  due to the POD% increase from 60% to 80%. since the system already recognized 600,000 SAP in the previous stage run. The system will post only additional (delta) of 200,000 SAR as extra provision in the current period.scheduled General Ledger jobs

SAP posts an additional impairment of 200,000 SAR to the Customer Reconciliation GL Account. This increases the total provision to 800,000 SAR, leaving a net receivable balance of 200,000 SAR.

Receivables Recon GL Account

D) Customer Write-Off Processing

On 15.12.2026. it’s Confirmed that the customer has gone bankrupt and will not make any payment, therefore in this stage the remaining balance must be fully written off. The AR department uses the Write Off Receivables app to clear the outstanding customer open amount and record the final loss in the system.

Open Write Off Receivables ID F6728

Write Off Receivables ID F6728

After selecting the customer account, SAP displays all open receivable items. Select the outstanding invoice identified for write-off and choose Post to complete the write-off process and record the transaction in the system.

Write Off Receivables ID F6728

Financial Posting: Debit ECL Expense G/L Account and Credit Customer Account (ABC).

Write Off Financial Impact

E) Final Impairment Run – Revaluation & Clearing

During testing, we notices that the Credit-Risk-Based Impairment run must be executed after the final write-off transaction. Otherwise, impairment balances may remain in the reconciliation account and require additional adjustments. Therefore, the previously recognized ECL amount of 800,000 SAR is reversed, ensuring that all balances are fully cleared and updated correctly.

The open item amount for the customer becomes zero against the full Expected Credit Loss expense amounting to 1 million SAR.

 

The Post Credit-Risk-Based Impairment job is executed again using the Schedule General Ledger Jobs app. The system reverses the previously recognized impairment amount.

Scheduled General Ledger jobs

Customer Reconciliation G/L Account is fully cleared after the Credit-Risk-Based Impairment run. SAP reverses the previously recognized impairment postings, ensuring that the reconciliation account balance is correctly aligned following the customer write-off.

Receivables Recon GL Account

The ECL Expense G/L Account is fully cleared through the impairment revaluation run. SAP reverses the previously recognized impairment postings and retains only the final write-off amount.

ECL (P&L) GL Account

Conclusion

SAP S/4HANA automates the Credit Risk Based Impairment process in compliance with IFRS9 by assessing customer risk classification ,probability of default percentage and aging analysis. Starts with outstanding invoice until the balance is written off.

This end to end process helps organizations maintain accurate balance sheet and P&L reporting while improving visibility into customer credit risk. 

In Part 2, I will explain the configuration steps by step and provide detailed system setup.
Stay tuned and follow for the next part of the series.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Introducing Our New Reply Progression Badges and a Color Refresh!
entry.content:
The votes are in and I’m excited to announce our winning colors!

Teal, Mango, Green, Indigo and Raspberry

A big thank you to everyone who took the time to share their favorite colors. It was great to see such strong engagement and thoughtful preferences across the community!

We explored a few different ways of counting votes (including ranked and normalized approaches), and the outcome was consistent across all methods, which gives us  confidence that these truly reflect the community’s preferences.

These colors will be making their way into the badge experience as part of the refresh. Stay tuned as they roll out!

Thanks again for helping shape this, this is the kind of community input that makes these updates more meaningful.

entry.title: BPマスタのFI得意先タブにある「振込人名」の表示順について
entry.content:
お世話になっております。
BPマスタのFI得意先タブにある「振込人名」の表示順について、SAP標準仕様を確認させてください。
現在、振込人名の入力項目は、以下のように「先頭数字」と「振込人名」の2つの要素で構成されている認識です。
例：
1　振込人名111
2　振込人名222
3　振込人名333
当初、複数の振込人名を登録した場合、表示順は先頭数字の昇順になると想定しておりました。
想定していた表示順：
1　振込人名111
2　振込人名222
3　振込人名333
しかし、実機で確認したところ、表示順は先頭数字の順番ではなく、入力・保存した順番で表示されているように見受けられます。
例えば、以下の順番で振込人名を入力・保存した場合、表示結果は下記の通りとなりました。
【確認例】
「3　333」を入力して保存表示結果：
3　333
続けて「2　222」を入力して保存表示結果：
3　333
2　222
続けて「1　111」を入力して保存表示結果：
3　333
2　222
1　111
続けて「4　444」を入力して保存表示結果：
3　333
2　222
1　111
4　444
上記の結果より、振込人名の表示順は、先頭数字の昇順ではなく、入力順または登録順に従って表示されているように見えます。
つきましては、以下についてご確認をお願いいたします。
1. BPマスタのFI得意先タブにおける振込人名の表示順は、「先頭数字」はソートキーではなく、SAP標準仕様として「入力順／登録順」で表示される認識で正しいでしょうか。

お手数をおかけしますが、ご確認のほどよろしくお願いいたします。

entry.title: Re: how to apply download S4 hana cloud ES all data
entry.content:
Dear Sender:

To extract and download system data, you can use the app "Customer Data Return".

To execute Customer Data Return, you need to:

  1. Trigger file preparation: This is carried out by the extraction user.

    For more information, see Working with the Customer Data Return - Extraction App.

  2. Trigger file download: This is carried out by the provisioning user.

    For more information, see Working with the Customer Data Return - Provisioning App.

You can find more information in the Help Portal below with this app:

Customer Data Return | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Anlegen von OSS Meldungen für SF
entry.content:
Der Testuser SPHONE_NUMBER hat das Berechtigungspaket Standard User (intern) und Einzelberechtigungen für das Anlegen von OSS Meldungen für Success Factor. Als Einzelberechtigung ist nicht 'Diplay Cloud Data' auswählbar. 

Wir wollen nicht die Cloud-Administrator PERSON_NAME zuweisen, da diese User kleine Cloud Administratoren sein sollen, sondern ausschließlich berechtigt sein sollen, um OSS Meldung für SF anzulegen.

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Anlegen von OSS Meldungen für SF
entry.content:
Regarding your issue, according to the translation, I understand you have an issue that could not assign "display cloud data " authorization on SF installation to S-user *.

As I checked in our backend, the SF installation is *.

The core logic of SAP for Me User Management is that an administrator can only manage authorizations they themselves hold.
Regarding this issue, please verify whether the admin S‑user you are currently using possesses the Display Cloud Data authorization on installation *.
Below is the list of admin users within your company who currently hold this authorization. You may reach out to them to grant the required access to test S‑user *.

entry.title: Re: Investment Boost: A significant step to promote economic growth
entry.content:
Hi Bino, 

Just testing this before I release this to our Finance team and It appears that NIBD and NIBS are the same. NIBS should be straight line but is is calculating diminishing value. 

These first three are for NIBD for an asset purchased today (30 June balance date) for $10,000. 

 

These next 3 are for NIBS for but the depreciation calculation is the same as NIBD when it should be straight line. 

 

 

Likely the indicator is missing from the Percent from Remaining Life field. You might want to check NIMS as well. 

Please let me know what you find. 

Thanks,

James

entry.title: Unable to expose RAP OData V4 UI app in Fiori Launchpad (not visible in Custom Catalog Extensions)
entry.content:
Hi experts,

I am currently working on a RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming) application in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (ABAP Cloud / Developer Extensibility), and I am facing an issue when trying to expose the app in the Fiori Launchpad. 

What I have implemented

  • RAP Business Object:

    • CDS Interface View (ZI_*)
    • Projection View (ZC_*)
    • Behavior Definition (with extensible)
    • Behavior Implementation

  • Service exposure:

    • Service Definition (ZSD_SO_UPLOAD_LOG)
    • Service Binding (OData V4 – UI)
    • Service successfully Published
    • UI preview works correctly in ADT

  • I can access the app directly via URL:

Steps already performed

  • Added @AbapCatalog.extensibility.extensible: true
  • Added extensible to Behavior Definition (BDEF)
  • Attempted API Release (C0 contract)
  • Activated and republished Service Binding
  • Verified Service Binding → UI works in preview

IAM / Catalog setup

  • Business Catalog created: ZBC_FILE_UPLOAD_SO
  • IAM App as Custom Inbound Service is generated
  • IAM App External App is also created : ZIAM_SO_FILE_UPLOAD_EXT
  • Catalog assignment is done

  •  

     

Issue

  • RAP UI app is NOT visible in “Custom Catalog Extensions”
  • Only Custom Business Objects appear there
  • When clicking the assigned IAM App, system throws:
Resource not found for segment 'aps_iam_app_ddlType'
  • The business catalog exists in Fiori Launchpad, but:
    • The extension entry cannot be opened
    • No Launchpad tile is generated

    •  

       

Reference Guide Followed

I followed this SAP blog:

https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-and-automation-blog-posts/quickly-generate-and-deploy-sap-fiori-apps-from-abap-development-tools-for/ba-p/14116822

However, I’m not able to achieve the same result in my system.

Questions

  1. Is it possible to expose RAP OData V4 UI apps in Fiori Launchpad without SAP BTP / BAS in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?

  2. Should RAP apps appear automatically in:

    • Custom Catalog Extensions ? OR
    • Is another registration step required?

  3. Is additional configuration required, such as:

    • Fiori App Descriptor creation?
    • Manual IAM setup?
    • Admin configuration?

  4. Is this limitation due to:

    • Tenant restrictions?
    • Missing roles or features?

 

 Any guidance would be greatly appreciated. Thank you!

entry.title: Opening Content Server Documents On the Internet
entry.content:
Hi Team,

We have recently enabled the FLP over the internet using a WAF which is connected to our RISE environment.

So we have a user on the public internet that clicks on the URL which points to the WAF- it then sends to the web dispatcher and then it sends to the S/4 HANA. All the transactions is working fine but when we open documents from the content server which is hosted by the servers on the customer network we are getting hostname unknown on the internet because what happens is the content server returns the content using the content server url and not the URL we used to launch FLP causing the hostname unknown. We dont want to expose the content server to public either , is there anyway we can set this up.

Any guidance on this?

entry.title: Re: Impairment Posting Error After Missing Fiscal Year Balance Carryforward
entry.content:
Thank you for the helpful response regarding the sequential execution of fiscal year balance carryforward (FAGLGVTR). I have two remaining questions that were not fully addressed:

Question 1: Is running the fiscal year balance carryforward sequentially for the two missing fiscal years (e.g., FY2023 → FY2024 → FY2025) the only available solution to resolve the impairment posting error in Asset Accounting on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition? Or are there any alternative approaches that could allow the impairment posting (reducing net book value from 1 JPY to 0 JPY) to be processed without completing the missing year-end close steps?

Question 2: If there is no workaround and the sequential year-end close is mandatory, could you confirm whether it is technically safe and supported to execute the balance carryforward retroactively for closed fiscal years in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition — specifically, are there any known risks, data inconsistencies, or additional steps (e.g., re-opening periods, re-running depreciation) that should be considered before proceeding?

We are operating on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, so any guidance specific to this environment (as opposed to on-premise or Private Cloud) would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Blog#3: How mandatory field checks are used and configured in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
In my last post, I shared how Cross Landscape Distribution (XLD) can be brought directly into SAP Cloud ALM

This time it is about something even more fundamental for data quality: Mandatory Field Checks. Sounds simple, but think about what that means. You have designed a process to be used in SAP Cloud ALM and the field "Scope" is essential for your concept to work. But how do you make sure that the users are really maintaining the field if SAP Cloud ALM is not enforcing it? Nobody will notice right away, but you will find out later, when the data is needed somewhere else, for example in a report or in an audit.

 

 

Let's be specific: A Requirement with Scope and Company Code as mandatory fields

In standard SAP Cloud ALM, you can find missing fields only afterwards when you check manually. By then, the data is already in the system, and someone has to clean it up.

Here is a concrete example from one of my projects. In the "Additional Information" area, we need two fields to be mandatory when a Requirement is saved: "Scope" (standard field) and "Company Code" (custom field which was added with pony).
As seen in the screenshot below, the two fields are marked in red and at the same time the "Save" and "Save & Close" buttons are greyed out. So the user cannot save the Requirement until both fields are filled. The check is preventive, not reactive.

 

Mandatory field check for a Requirement

 

When we fill the mandatory fields (Scope and Company Code), the red marking disappears, and the Save buttons become available again. We can create the Requirement and continue.

 

What can be checked, not only single mandatory fields but much more

The example above is the simplest case. With pony you can do much more on Requirements, User Stories and Features:

  • Check single mandatory fields, depending on context
  • Validate field combinations, for example: if "Change Type" is "Urgent", then "Justification" must be filled
  • Secure status transitions, so a status change is only allowed when all required fields are completed
  • Apply your own governance rules in addition to the standard ones
  • Limit a rule to a specific project, workstream or other condition
  • Limit a rule to specific roles, for example a check that applies only to developers and not to administrators

 

All of this works on the standard SAP Cloud ALM screens.

 

How it is configured, without code

Mandatory field checks are setup and configured with a with a graphical editor. No developer required.

In the pony administration, we have a "Rules" tile. There, we see all the rules that are configured for our tenant, for example "Scope mandatory" and "Company Code mandatory".

 

Library for pony rules

 

A rule in pony is built in three steps:

  • Step 1 - What should be validated: the object (e.g., Requirement) and the field that is required (e.g., Company Code)
  • Step 2 - When and where the rule applies: for example on the Save and Save & Close buttons. Additional conditions can narrow it down further, for example "only for project X" or "only when workstream Y is used"
  • Step 3 - Who is affected: the rule can apply to all users or only to specific roles, for example only to developers

Finally, we give the rule a name and enable it. On the right side of the editor, a help section explains the structure once more.

Details of a pony rule

A click on Apply and the rule is active. Back in SAP Cloud ALM, the Scope and the Company Code are marked as mandatory, and the Requirement can no longer be saved without them.

 

In summary: mandatory field rules can be enforced directly inside SAP Cloud ALM. The checks are built into the standard UI, marks missing fields in red, and disables the Save action until the data is complete. Mandatory field rules are no longer a question of discipline. They are a question of configuration.

Thanks for reading. If you have your own "the report or the process broke because the field was empty" story, drop it in the comments.

In my next post, I will share how pony can make fields display only, so the data stays visible to everyone but cannot be changed by users who should not be allowed to change it.

entry.title: Query Draft in RAP
entry.content:
Introduction

The Draft capability in SAP RAP enables users to create, modify, and save business data without immediately affecting active records. This feature significantly improves the user experience by allowing work-in-progress changes to be stored and resumed later. 

In a standard draft-enabled RAP application, when a user switches to Edit mode, the active data is copied into the draft table and all subsequent operations are performed on the draft instance. However, one important limitation is that access controls and authorization checks cannot be directly applied to a database table. 

SAP introduced the Draft Query View . A Draft Query View is a CDS view created on top of the draft table and assigned in the behavior definition using the query addition. When RAP reads draft data, it uses this CDS view instead of directly accessing the draft table. This allows developers to apply DCL rules, authorization checks, and additional filtering logic specifically for draft instances. 

what is Query Draft?

A draft query view can be used to define read access limitations to the draft data using data control language(DCL) The DCL access restrictions defined for the CDS view entities selecting from the active database table are not applied to the draft data. A draft query view allows the definition of read access restrictions for draft data.

Implementation steps

Step 1 : Create  a Data Base Table

@EndUserText.label : 'SALES ORDER DATA'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zte_t_salesdata {

  key mandt       : abap.clnt not null;
  key seller_id   : abap.char(5) not null;
  name            : abap.char(20);
  avaliblity      : abap_boolean;
  created_at      : timestampl;
  last_changed_at : timestampl;

}

Step 2 : Create root CDS view for table

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'SALES DATA'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZTE_I_SALESDATA as select from zte_t_salesdata
{
    key seller_id as SellerId,
    name as Name,
    avaliblity as Avaliblity,
    created_at as CreatedAt,
    last_changed_at as LastChangedAt
}

Step 3: create projection view

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'SALES DATA'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZTE_C_SALESDATA
  provider contract transactional_query as projection on ZTE_I_SALESDATA
{
@UI.facet: [{ id: 'salesdetails', 
position: 1, 
label: 'SALES DATA', 
type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE 
} ]
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 1, label : 'SELLER ID' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 1, label : 'SELLER ID' }]
    key SellerId,
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 2, label : 'SELLER Name' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 2, label : 'SELLER Name' }]
    Name,
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 3, label : 'Avalilabity' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 3, label : 'Avalilabity' }]
    Avaliblity,
    @ui.lineItem: [{ position: 4, label : 'Created At' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 4, label : 'Created At' }]
    CreatedAt,
      @ui.lineItem: [{ position: 5, label : 'LastChangedAt' }] 
@UI.identification: [{ position: 5, label : 'LastChangedAt' }]
    LastChangedAt
}

Step 4 :  Create Behavior Definition 

  • Here create a Draft  
  • After creating draft table create a interface view top of draft table 
  • Will declaring the draft table use the QUERY keyword and provide the interface view name of the draft table

 Draft Data base Table

@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZTE_I_SALESDATA'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zte_sales_d {

  key mandt     : mandt not null;
  key sellerid  : abap.char(5) not null;
  name          : abap.char(20);
  avaliblity    : abap_boolean;
  createdat     : timestampl;
  lastchangedat : timestampl;
  "%admin"      : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;

}

Interface view of Draft data base table

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'Draft sales data'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZTE_I_SALESDATA_D as select from zte_sales_d
{
    key sellerid as Sellerid,
    name as Name,
    avaliblity as Avaliblity,
    createdat as Createdat,
    lastchangedat as Lastchangedat,
    draftentitycreationdatetime as Draftentitycreationdatetime,
    draftentitylastchangedatetime as Draftentitylastchangedatetime,
    draftadministrativedatauuid as Draftadministrativedatauuid,
    draftentityoperationcode as Draftentityoperationcode,
    hasactiveentity as Hasactiveentity,
    draftfieldchanges as Draftfieldchanges
}

Step 5 : Define Projection behavior 

projection;
strict ( 2 );
use draft;

define behavior for ZTE_C_SALESDATA //alias <alias_name>
{
  use create;
  use update;
  use delete;

  use action Activate;
  use action Resume;
  use action Discard;
  use action Prepare;
  use action Edit;
}

Step 6 :  Create access control for both interface view(main interface view and draft interface view)

Access control for main Interface view

@EndUserText.label: 'Sales Data'
@MappingRole: true
define role ZTE_R_SALESDATA {
    grant
        select
            on
                ZTE_I_SALESDATA
                    where
                       avaliblity = 'X';
                        
}

Access control for draft interface view

@EndUserText.label: 'Sales Data Draft'
@MappingRole: true
define role ZTE_R_SALESDATA_D {
    grant
        select
            on
                ZTE_I_SALESDATA_D
                    where
                       Avaliblity is initial;
                        
}

Step 7 : Create Service definition and service binding

Service Definition

@EndUserText.label: 'sales data'
define service Zsd_te_SalesData {
  expose ZTE_C_SALESDATA;
}

 Service Binding

The Binding Type has to be ODATA V4

Result

If the Field Availability is  'X'  then the data will be shown as Saved Version and cannot edit the record.

If the Field Availability is Not  'X'  then the data will be shown as Draft and can edit the record.

Conclusion

Draft Query in SAP RAP allows controlled access to draft data by placing a CDS view over the draft table and assigning it via the query addition in the behavior definition.

Key benefits:

  • Apply authorization checks, DCL rules, and custom filters specifically for drafts
  • Draft and active entities can follow independent visibility rules

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: Hidden Gems – Fine-Tuning Fraud Detection Strategies in SAP BIS
entry.content:
By its very nature, fraud is dynamic. So, a static strategy quickly becomes less accurate over time.

Designing effective fraud detection strategies is challenging because fraud tactics evolve constantly, because the data to monitor is massive and comes in high-volume, and because fraud detection systems must strike a careful balance between identifying fraud and minimizing false positives—all while operating in real time and complying with privacy and regulatory requirements.

An effective fraud detection strategy typically combines various rules together, extended with machine learning for anomaly detection, to address both known fraud patterns and new, emerging threats.

As a result, ongoing performance monitoring and regular fine-tuning is essential to make sure the company adapts to changes in behaviour, evolving processes, and regulatory landscape.

And the secret ingredient to this successful recipe is the “regular fine-tuning”.

 

What is fine-tuning fraud detection strategies

 

In essence, this relates to adjusting an existing fraud model or set of fraud detection strategies, so it works better for an organization’s specific business and current fraud patterns.

This can be achieved by changing model weights, thresholds, creating and combining new detection methods, or adjusting fraud rule sensitivity based on new outcomes and investigator feedback. It is usually a continuous cycle: review performance, identify misses and false alerts, adjust, test, then redeploy.

Fine-tuning keeps the fraud system in equilibrium – robust enough to detect fraud while remaining flexible enough to avoid overwhelming investigators with excessive false alerts.

And this is where I would like to introduce a hidden gem embedded within SAP Business Integrity Screening since the very first day, but is not known widely enough in my humble opinion, the Calibration of Detection Strategies.

 

Calibration of Detection Strategies in SAP Business Integrity Screening

 

Let’s first take just one step back.

In case you are not familiar with SAP Business Integrity Screening, this solution helps organizations mitigate fraud risk, reduce losses, and improve the detection and prevention of anomalies. The short video below will provide a quick overview:

With this solution, fraud investigation teams can create detection methods with associated thresholds, weighing factors, etc. and then combine them into detection strategies. These then run automatically to surface anomalies that can then be further checked.

To make sure that these detection strategies are optimum, users can leverage the “Calibration of Detection Strategies” capability. This is a real-time simulation function for what-if analyses.

Step 1 – Review actual

In this first step, users get details on the baseline reference strategy to improve:

  • Number of confirmed, false positives, and unclassified alerts
  • Efficiency ratio of the detection strategy

Step 2 – Fine-tune the settings

In this second step, users can change settings such as:

  • Selection period for the data that is relevant for the simulation
  • Threshold data point of the strategy
  • Weighing factors and parameter values of each detection method
  • … and run the simulation

This will create a new tile with a new calculated efficiency ratio based on the following performance indicators:

 

KPI

Description

Confirmed

Number of alert items that would have been created and classified as Confirmed for detection objects that already have been identified as Confirmed in the actual data.

False Positives

Number of alert items that would have been created and classified as Confirmed for detection objects that already have been identified as False Alarm in the actual data.

New Alert Items

Number of alert items that will be created during simulation and that have not been created within the actual data.

Missed Alert Items

Number of alert items that were created by the strategy in the past but were not found during the current simulation.

Found Alert Items

Number of alert items that were both created by the strategy in the past and were found during the current simulation.

Risk Value of New Alert Items

Sum of all the risk values of the new alert items found during the current simulation that were not found by the strategy in the past.

Risk Value of Found Alert Items

Sum of all the risk values of the alert items that were both created by the strategy in the past and were found during the current simulation.

Risk Value of Missed Alert Items

Sum of all the risk values of the alert items that were created by the strategy in the past but were not found during the current simulation.

[optional] Step 3 – Get best fit suggestions

Of course, users can run as many simulations as they like until they get the best outcome for their requirements: reduce the number of positives, not miss any alerts, etc.

Should users also want some automated proposal, they can use the Find Best Values capability available. The system will then suggest the best values determined.

Step 4 – Roll it out

Once the detection strategy is adjusted to a satisfactory level, users can save the new set of settings.

By saving the results of the calibration, the system creates a new detection strategy version with status inactive. It would then simply need to be activated so that it can be executed routinely.

 

See it in action

 

 

Benefits of fine-tuning detection strategies

 

Reduces false positives so fraud investigators can focus on what matters

Improves detection method with adaptive measures as behaviours or tactics change, which keeps the strategy relevant over time

 

Interested in learning more?

 

In addition to the section on the SAP Help Portal, there is also an SAP Learning Course focused exclusively on this solution – Exploring SAP Business Integrity Screening, with a dedicated section on “Monitoring and Improving Detection Quality”:

What about you, how does your company ensure that its detection strategies continue to perform well over time? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this blog.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 07 - Authorization annotations
entry.content:
Within the topic of security and data privacy and specifically within security, we have:

  • Authentication: verification of a user's identity
  • Authorization: checking what level of access a given user has

In other words, "who" a user is (authentication), and "how" that user can interact with the resources (authorization).

For authorization, CAP offers role-based access control facilities that revolve around a couple of annotations @requires and @restrict.

The @restrict annotation allows for complex definitions of privilege declarations each of which can combine the granting of access to roles according to specific conditions, in this pattern:

{
  grant:<events>,
  to:<roles>,
  where:<condition>
}

The @requires annotation is actually just a convenience shortcut to something that we might commonly express with a @restrict annotation; for example:

@requires: 'Viewer'

is the equivalent of

@restrict: [ { grant:'*', to:'Viewer' } ]

There's another security related annotation which is in common use, which is actually just another convenience shortcut for:

@restrict: [ { grant:'READ' } ]

What is the name of that annotation?

This is a question from the June Developer Challenge on CAP Knowledge. And don't forget: always submit your answer as a hash, on its own - read the Taking part section of the intro post for more info. At the end of today, this question will be updated with links to further reading on this topic.

entry.title: Extending CKM3N in S/4HANA: A Practical Approach for Multi-Material and Multi-Period Analysis
entry.content:
The CKM3N Limitation

Anyone who has worked with CKM3N in S/4HANA knows how valuable it is for Material Ledger analysis. However, when it comes to analyzing multiple materials or comparing different periods, the standard approach quickly becomes restrictive.

This limitation motivated me to explore a more analytical approach for Material Ledger consumption using CDS Views.

CKM3N Standard

Why This Becomes a Problem

In Product Costing and Material Ledger scenarios, it is very common to require comparative analysis across multiple materials and periods.

With CKM3N, this often means executing the transaction repeatedly, exporting data, and manually consolidating information.

This process can become time-consuming and makes it harder to identify cost trends efficiently.

A Different Approach Using CDS Views

The approach was to model Material Ledger data using CDS Views, preserving a structure similar to CKM3N while enabling collective analysis.

The objective was not to replace CKM3N, but to complement it with broader analytical capabilities.

  • Multiple materials
  • Multiple periods
  • Hierarchical visualization
  • Analytical consumption through different frontends

Multi-material Selection

Performance Considerations

Performance became a critical aspect due to the high volume of Material Ledger data.

To improve scalability, filter pushdown strategies and optimized CDS modeling were applied to ensure that filters were executed as early as possible.

This was particularly important when working with Material Ledger datasets and cost component split information.

Execution / Performance

Results

Although the current implementation was explored using an ALV-based visualization, the CDS-based model also enables alternative consumption patterns, such as Fiori applications or API exposure.
 
This flexibility allows the same analytical foundation to be reused across different scenarios depending on business needs.

ALV Table

Tree View

 To improve the analytical experience, a lightweight header inspired by Fiori design principles was introduced in the ALV output, displaying key indicators at runtime.

 
This allows users to quickly identify relevant insights without navigating through detailed line items, bringing a more modern and efficient interaction model even in a classic ALV context.

Final Thoughts

This exercise highlighted how analytical flexibility can significantly improve Material Ledger consumption in S/4HANA.

I am curious to understand how other teams are addressing similar CKM3N limitations.

👉Feel free to share your approach or connect to exchange ideas.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 03 - Declarative field control with @mandatory
Re: Header Note in My Purchase Requisitions (F1643A)
entry.content:
Hello @AnonymouslyM ,

generally, there are two different text field options in S/4HANA Public Cloud: Notes and Texts.

While texts are meant to be printed and communicated, notes are usually for internal communication only.

Therefore, it would be logically correct to not print header notes on PR / PO output.

 

However, on item level there are different text fields available (Item Text, Delivery Text, Material PO Text). These values can be transmitted to follow-on documents like the PO.

 

So the workaround is: Instead of using Header Notes try using Item Texts.

 

I hope this helps.

Best regards,

Kerisha

 

Kerisha Lawrence

Public Cloud | IAM, Procurement, Post Go-Live

https://www.linkedin.com/in/kerisha-lawrence

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud, on AVC Need to derive BOM quantity...
entry.content:
Hi, @Lord_Reaver 

  • What is the correct modeling approach?  // use/create reference char and assign procedure to BOM item to modify the item quantity.

  • Should this be done via Variable-Size Item reference characteristic? // in your case it's not necessary to go variable-size, just simple reference char.

  • Is Quantity Formula supported? // in your case it's not necessary to go variable-size, just simple reference char.

eg.

1. create a reference char name REF_QTY,  in additional data tab, put table STPO and field MENGE

2. in that procedure to assign to BOM item,  instead of ROMS1 = $ROOT.Z_LONGITUD, use below:

    

$self.REF_QTY = $parent.Z_LONGITUD

better to use $parent to indicate the direct header material (the finished product).  use $root may point to a different material in multi-level BOM configuration.

3. you should have another procedure in finished product's profile to determine Z_LONGITUD

entry.title: Automating Probation Process Management in SAP SuccessFactors Using Integration Center
entry.content:
Introduction:

Probation process usually varies from business to business depending on the policy. It is one of the most important phases as it decides the employment confirmation. One of our customers raised a concern about their major manual probation activities and wanted to automate it via SAP SuccessFactors. They wanted to achieve it without any custom developments or CPI involvement.

Thus, the solution is proposed and implemented successfully to them using SAP SuccessFactors standard functionalities of Employee Central, Integration Center, Reporting, Off-cycle event batch and, alerts and notification only.

Business Need:

Firstly, will explain the business requirement from customer. The customer already has probation evaluation form which is launched automatically based on hired date as per manage scheduled reviews.

But post evaluation, HR team manually intervenes in analysing the reports to identify the final decision of every document, updating job information with probation status, handles probation extensions, rolling out probation emails.

Thus, they want to automate the below using standard SuccessFactors capabilities.

  • Once a probation evaluation form is completed, the system should automatically identify the final decision.
  • If the employee successfully completes probation, the probation status should be automatically updated in Job Information.
  • Employees should receive a confirmation email upon probation completion. HR stakeholders should be copied in the communication for visibility and tracking.
  • Else if the final decision is to extend probation, the updated probation end date and probation status should be automatically updated in Job Information.
  • The system should automatically launch a Probation Extension Evaluation Form 30 days before the extended probation period ends.

Solution Overview:

The implementation of the probation automation process includes the below steps.

  1. Generating and Scheduling Probation Completion and Extension Reports to SFTP.
  2. Capturing Final Decision in Employment Details.
  3. Updating Job Information based on the final probation.
  4. Triggering employee and HR notifications.
  5. Automatically launching probation extension evaluation forms when required.

 

Solution Implementation:

Prerequisite: Probation end date must be defaulted in employee's job information during hiring, using business rule. 

For better understanding: In the referred screenshots of this blog, second probation end date refers the actual end date of probation period before extension. As the customer has two phases in the probation period, it is referred as second.

Step 1: Generating and Scheduling Probation Completion and Extension Reports to SFTP

The automation process begins with creating and scheduling separate reports for probation completion and probation extension. These reports identify only completed probation evaluation forms, which ensures only the finalized evaluation forms are processed.

Each report captures the key information required processing like,

  • Subject User id
  • Form Title
  • Form Status
  • Final Decision (Section Custom Field)

The scheduled reports run automatically and generate the output file in csv format to the SFTP destination. This file will be the source of data for further process and integration activities.

Final decision captured in the reports for reference,

Probation completion report:

Probation extension report:

Step 2: Capturing Final Decision in Employment Details

Since employmentinfo(Employment Details) is non-effective dated object, it plays the role of intermediate staging area before updating the job information directly.

Thus, to record the final decision of probation evaluation forms, two custom fields are created in Employment Information as below.

  • Probation extension period – to record the period of probation extension if probation extended
  • Probation completion – to record the employment confirmation if probation is completed

Integration Center jobs are created individually for fetching and processing the two reports separately.

These jobs fetch the respective reports from the SFTP location, process and updates the custom fields in Employment information of the subject employees.

Mapping for IC job updating Probation extension field:

Mapping for IC job updating Probation completion field:

Step 3: Updating Job Information based on the final probation decision

Subsequent Integration Center jobs are created for updating job information based on the final decision captured in Employment Details.

These jobs identify those employees whose employment details are captured with final decisions and job information is yet to be updated with probation status.

Note: Ensure the probation event reasons are causing no errors in ECP replication.

Case 1: Integration Center Job – Posting Probation Completion Event:

If Employment Details have captured the probation completion, this job will post the probation completion as follows.

  • A Job Information event is posted with probation completion as event reason
  • Probation status is updated to Confirmed.
  • Probation End Date (calculated based on extension if any) is used as the event effective date.

Below are filters to fetch the records with current date as probation end date or probation extension end date. This helps in processing necessary records only and avoid duplicate posting of events. Clear the time-based filters if any.

Event posted for probation completion in Job Information:

Case 2: Integration Center Job – Posting probation extension event

If Employment Details have captured the probation extension period, this job will post the probation completion as follows.

  • A Job Information event is posted with probation extension as event reason
  • Probation status is updated to Extended.
  • Extended probation end date is updated in Job Information.
  • Probation End Date is used as the event effective date

Below are filters to fetch the records with current date as probation end date. This helps in processing necessary records only and avoid duplicate posting of events. Clear the time-based filters if any.

Event posted for probation extension in Job Information:

Step 4: Triggering employee and HR notifications.

Once the probation completion event is posted, email notification must be sent automatically to the subject employee, HR stakeholders to ensure timely communication to the stakeholders.

To achieve this, the below business rule is associated with the Job Information portlet and configured with the Save Alert event.

Step 5: Automatically launching probation extension evaluation forms when required.

For employees whose probation has been extended, probation extension evaluation form must be launched automatically 30 days prior to the end of probation extension.

An Off-Cycle Event Batch job runs daily and evaluates employees with an active probation extension.

The batch process checks whether the employee is approaching the extension completion date. This ensures extension reviews are initiated at the appropriate time

When the employee reaches 30 days before the extended probation end date:

  • The system automatically launches a Probation Extension Evaluation Form.
  • The launch is driven through the configured PM Form field.

Conclusion:

Probation process usually involves many manual activities, frequent monitoring and delayed communication affecting the timely process. This scalable and user-friendly solution using standard SAP SuccessFactors functionalities, ensures streamlined and automated probation management while ensuring timely actions and accurate employee records.

entry.title: How to Create an Internal Agreement in SAP TM Step by Step Guide
entry.content:
Internal Agreement is used to support intercompany settlement between two company entities. In a typical setup, one company acts as the ordering entity, while another company executes the transportation activity.

The Internal Agreement helps to determine the correct charges between these two entities.

Step1: Create via Manage Internal Agreements Fiori app with Agreement Type, Validity (From/To), Currency and leave Status as In Process initially.

 




Step2: Assign the service provider org unit (Company B: executing side Freight Order) and the business partner for the purchasing org (Company A: ordering side Forwarding Order) this is what the system matches with freight Order.

 

 

Step3: Add Calculation Sheet and relevant charges types and release with rate tables.

Conclusion:

Creating an Internal Agreement in SAP TM is a small configuration activity, but it has a major impact on intercompany settlement. The most important part is maintaining the correct relationship between the executing company and the ordering company.

entry.title: Remaining value in the asset even after completion of useful life
entry.content:
We have certain assets in our system, wherein their useful life has been completed, but there is still some value that remains in the asset, we use the ‘day wise depreciation’ method.

Now the depreciation on the said asset was last run in FY 2023, now the system is not allowing us to calculate the unplanned depreciation, since the FY 2023 is already closed & we cannot keep more than 2 fiscal years open in the system.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 02 - Content for common reuse types
IAM App & Successor in Same Business Role? How to Fix It (Part 2/2)
entry.content:
Quick Solution

If you see the warning:

“The assignment of an IAM app and its successor to the same business role is invalid…”

👉   The root cause is active deprecated IAM apps with successors.

✔️   Fix: Deactivate or remove all deprecated IAM apps in the role → the warning disappears immediately.

When do you need this?

  • You are maintaining business roles after an upgrade
  • You see the IAM successor warning
    • More apps will be deprecated
    • More successor conflicts will occurThis step is critical before upcoming releases (e.g. 2608):
    • Cleaning up early avoids: missing apps, confusion for business users, unnecessary troubleshooting

Background (Part 1 Recap)

In Part 1, I showed why this warning should not be ignored:

👉   It can have real functional impact (e.g. successor apps not appearing)

➡️   In Part 2, here is the practical fix

Step-by-Step: Remove the Warning

1. Open Business Role

  • Go to Maintain Business Roles
  • Open the relevant role
  • Switch to Edit Mode

2. Navigate to Upgrade Adjustments

  • Click on “Manage Changes After Upgrade”
  • Open “Deprecated IAM Apps”

➡️    Here’s the key insight: All listed deprecated apps with successors are causing the warning

 

3. Verify Successor Apps

Before making changes:

✅   Ensure the successor app is already active

👉   Important to avoid access issues!

4. Deactivate Deprecated Apps

  • Go to tab “IAM Apps”
  • Search for the IAM App ID of the deprecated app
  • Set the app to Inactive
  • Repeat for all affected apps

5. Alternative: Remove via Business Catalog

Depending on the assignment type, you can also:

  • Remove the business catalog
  • Or remove the individual app assignment

Result

After deactivating/removing all deprecated IAM apps: The warning disappears immediately

Important Note

Switching from a deprecated app to its successor may impact users:

  • different UI
  • changed functionality
  • different behavior

Recommendation:   1️⃣   Align changes with affected users   2️⃣  Communicate before activation

💬  Your Experience?

Have you already cleaned up deprecated IAM apps in your business roles?

Did you notice any unexpected behavior during the transition?

Feel free to share - and if this guide helped, a like 👍 is always appreciated!

Kerisha Lawrence

Public Cloud | IAM, Procurement, Post Go-Live

https://www.linkedin.com/in/kerisha-lawrence

entry.title: Re: deletion logs of table BUT0ID
entry.content:
Hi,

If the changes are not recorded in the CDHDR and CDPOS tables, they will not be reflected in the change history.

I suspect the issue may be with the selection criteria being used when querying the CD* tables. Please use:

  • Object Class: BUPA_BUP
  • Object ID: The 10-digit Business Partner (BP) number

If the BP number consists entirely of numeric characters, prefix it with an asterisk (*) when entering the Object ID, as the Object ID field expects the BP number in a strict 10-digit format.

Regards,
Shwetha

entry.title: Re: SAP ASE Internals
entry.content:
@sladebe 

> In my RedHat/Centos version of Linux, when running ASE in threaded mode, I see this in Linux 
there are clearly Linux light weight processes (LWP) for each thread in ASE
No.  You are looking at the o/s utility (ps), the ASE Threads are visible inside ASE, not outside in the o/s.  Those LWPs are the [3] client connections to ASE, running as Sub-Processes, under the ASE Server [single] Process.  P & T Basics p31.  Eg 3 x isql sessions, unfortunately on the same host machine.

> My server has threads configured the following way:
No. you are looking at ThreadPools, not Threads.  syb_default_pool is the ThreadPool that runs Engine Threads (client tasks; spids).  My doc, page 9.

If you want Threads, do:
isql> sp_helpthread syb_default_pool

> So sp_helpthread is showing [a count of] 15 threads.
Yes, that there are 15 Threads, not the Threads themselves..

> Interestingly, at the Linux level, I see 21 threads/LWPs.
So what.  (Already explained above.)

> Probably a few ASE system threads not showing in sp_helpthread,
Please, this is IT, hopefully we are qualified technical people, we need to be precise and clear, arithmetic works, we are not baristas, where the coffee beans go missing, somehow, somewhere, and its "fine".

> that's fine.

  1. If you can't find something, or the arithmetic does not add up, it is definitely not "fine." 
    The ASE Threads are visible in ASE/sp_helpthread <poolname>,
    the os/Threads are visible in Linux/ps.
  2. You are conflating ASE Engine Threads and Tasks (spids).  Please stop. 
    They are not the same, please read my doc carefully, every word counts.  Page 2 first, and only when you fully understand that, page 9.  
  3. If you still can't differentiate ASE Engine Threads and Tasks (spids), I am afraid I cannot help you.

> I generally think of lightweight processes in Linux as OS implemented threads.   From what I'm reading online, threads in Linux are actually processes which happen to share some resources with the parent "thread" (see Light-weight process (Wikipedia))

  1. Don't worry about what you think, just learn about objective reality (we are scientific, yes ?).  Your thinking becomes relevant only when your mind conforms to objective reality.  I can best help you by declaring what ASE does (objective reality) and by not addressing your thinking.
  2. Don't read online, the internet is a cesspool, people with two fingers and a bit of connective tissue eagerly post the most absurd bottom-up solutions, blissfully ignorant of the fact that they are ignorant (read the Dunning-Krüger Effect).  Wikipedia is particular bad because it is (a) heavily biased, and (b) provies masses of irrelevant detail without proper definitions, leaving one confsued and unresolved in the Middle.  Here it gives themost generic description (not definition) of an LWP.
    • ASE LWPs are a particular implementation that is not known to Wiki.
    • you have purchased SAP/ASE, it comes with a full set of manuals. 
      SAP ASE Books Online
      Download the PDFs, read every TableOfContent, so that you know what all the subjects are, and where to find them when you need to.
    • read the ASE manuals and nothing but the ASE manuals
    • My docs are not a substitute for the manuals, rather they bring together all the info on a subject that is otherwise situated in bits and pieces throughout various manuals
    • if there is anything that you do not fully understand, stop, and ask a specific question here

Repeating, there are, discretely:

  • os/Processes = Engine Threads
  • os/Sub-Processes = Client connections within (or "under") an EngineThread
    an EngineThread executes a spid, until it ContextSwitches, and then executes the next spid, etc
  • ASE/ThreadPool x 3
    • ASE/Thread Pool/syb_default_pool =Engine Thread
  • ASE/Task (spid) = ASE Internal object that is executed by an Engine Thread
    much like on Linux, a pid is an internal object executed by an os/Thread
    NB. The thing that is executing, and the thing that is being executed by the thing that does the executing, cannot be the same thing.

> FYI, you can run "top -H" to see ASE activity at the lightweight thread level.
Not really.  Not to say that is not useful, but that is at the os level only, and concerns ASE overall, it says nothing about  spids, or a particular spid that you are trying to investigate.  And even for the full ASE (all Threads and all LWPs), you need all the Linux metrics, taken together., not just one.

> Generally speaking, I was trying to say that sp_sysmon shows things from the engine/thread point of view,
Yes.

>not from a user task (spid) point of view.
Yes.

> Ie., you can see that the overall ASE server is busy or now,
Yes.  (But the server is mostly idle, waiting for I/Os to complete.)

>but it's hard to tell what an individual spid is bottlenecked on.
Yes.  sp_symons provide rather excellent server metrics.  Why are you looking at the server or server metrics when you are trying to investigate a Task (spid) ???

Immediate Answer

To understand what a Task (spid) does, how it will be executed (eg. what I/Os it will cause; etc),

  1. first and foremost, use
    sp_who <spid>
    that is its purpose, to provide info about the operation of a current spid
    • learn all the statuses, and what they mean

  2. obtain the Query Plan to see what the spid is doing.  Do (session settings, not SQL commands):
    isql> SET SHOWPLAN ON

  3. After having understood what actions your spid is going to cause in the server, to investigate the performance of a spid, do:
    isql> SET STATISTICS IO ON
    isql> SET STATISTICS TIME ON

Long Term Answer

  • There are great ASE facilities to monitor the server, as well as spid activity, overall and particular, read up on it.  Monitoring (MDA) tables.  Much overhead, depending on what you capture.
    • I don't have need for them because I established a suite monitoring for Sybase (eg. decades before the advent of MDA tables, sp_sysmon is quite adequate for me, and zero overhead.  Another example, presented by the hour.
  • There are great third party monitoring tools as well.  Expensive but brilliant.  Bradmark Surveillance. top shelf for ASE server level, and does a decent job for spids as well.
  • If your application is third party (ie. you do not have the source code for the spid or sproc or whatever), set up ASE Auditing, and capture what it is doing, then the [1][2] above will make sense.

> It's possible to get some idea what individual spids are doing using the "appmgmt" option within sp_sysmon:
Not a snowball's chance in hell. 

Before you attempt to interpret an sp_symon,

  1. make sure the sample you are collecting is relevant to your investigation (right now it is not ... looking at server metrics for task (spid) metrics ... there are hundreds or whatever Tasks running plus server internal tasks
  2. please read the P&T/Monitoring manual, so that you (a) know what you are looking at, and (b) how to interpret it
  3. "Application Management" is an ASE facility, which you have not configured, and are not using, that section is irrelevant to you, and has nothing to do with a spid or the spid that you are investigating

> Where, according to the docs,
Ok, so you are looking at the manual, but you have picked the detail under "Application Management" without reading the section on "Application Management", which states:

  • This section is useful if you use resource limits, or if you plan to tune applications by setting execution attributes and assigning engine affinity.

Repeating [3] above.

Please do not copy-paste the manuals, please link to them and specify a page.

> The way I'm reading this, "Idle" ticks only occur for a spid if it's scheduled. 
...<snip> all the way to ...
So this seems to say that a spid waiting for an I/O to complete shows up as "idle"

  1. Those metrics are relevant to the server, specifically the Application Management breakdown, not the spid
    you have not specified the parameters that you gave to sp_sysmon
  2. you need to understand the states (statuses) that a spid encounters, and what it means.  Again, start with sp_who, and read up on the statuses.
  3. Then the SET STATISTICS, etc, as explained above.

Wait.  If you are running a limited, controlled number of Tasks exclusively on the server and then trying to interpret the Application Management section, that is a really long-winded and bottom-up way to find out what the task is doing.  That is like running a high speed long-distance train, fully equipped with staff and gear, say 8 passenger carriages, with just one passenger, and collecting train stats to determine how the single passenger is travelling.  Further, how poorly or how well your SQL is written (performance-wise and quality-wise) seriously affects the way the Engine has to deal with it.

  • Go with the top-down, normal method explained above.  It will help you to progressively understand the server/EngineThread vs the Task (spid).
  • Nevertheless, since you are already in the guts of the sp_sysmon report, in whatever context you have, the Task Management section will give you slightly more relevant metrics: why the Task is being Context-Switched out by the EngineThread that is executing it.

> So this seems to say that a spid waiting for an I/O to complete shows up as "idle"
No.  Idle means the Task has not sent a batch of SQL to the sever.  The sp_who status is recv sleep.

In this non-applicable report, waiting for [all, not one] I/Os to complete shows up in the CPU section, as I/O Busy.  The sp_who status is sleeping.

Here is something to consider, probably too detailed without an explanation.

entry.title: Control output generation for Solution Order based on business needs using extensibility options
entry.content:
What is Solution Order in SAP? 
SOLUTION ORDER supports mixed items selling as items put together in a solution order and also as sets of items added as one bundle (called solution bundle). You can use solution orders to sell to your customers any combination of different products, such as physical goods, one-time services, long-running service contracts, subscription-based services, and project-based services.

To manage solution orders, you can use the Manage Solution Orders app, where one can release solution order items and trigger orchestration, creating follow-up documents having their own lifecycle and system checks. It is also possible to print out a solution order confirmation and send it to a customer.

Customer Requirement

The need is to maintain consistency of the solution sold for the customers in all external communications with them. Hence, it is needed that output generation for customers be controlled to be sent as and when needed from solution order and the follow up documents created from it.

For this blog, the example is to send an order confirmation from SOLUTION ORDER only. It should also be sent only when solution order is created and released successfully and the follow up documents of SALES ORDER, SERVICE CONTRACT AND SERVICE ORDER, are created without any errors. Errors considered here are credit check block for all follow-up service transactions and and incompletion check for the sales order document specifically.

Once the SOLUTION ORDER is released, the system creates SERVICE ORDER, SERVICE CONTRACT AND SALES ORDER all with a credit check block. Additionally, Incompletion  Status is triggered for the Sales Order based on the customer-defined incompletion procedure. Until these blocks are removed, no customer output or order confirmation should be generated or sent for the solution order. After the credit check block is removed from all follow-up documents via documented credit decisions and incompletion status is removed for the sales order, the system should automatically allow to print and send order confirmation to a customer, which will be normally happening via the scheduled application job.

1) Solution Design I_BUSINESSSOLUTIONORDERTP and create a custom filed yy1_documentprint. By default, this field will be blank and not visible to user it will be only set and used by print configuration to check eligibility of Solution Order To decide Print Or Not To Print

If the custom field needs to be extended to the application UI, then enable its usage for C_BUSSOLUTIONORDER.

2) Consume Local Event Create and Change for Sales Order, Service Order and Service Contract I_SalesOrderTP , I_ServiceOrderTP and I_ServiceContractTP

for details, please refer to Business Object Consumption | SAP Help Portal . These events are consumed to identify when a relevant business object is created or changed, so that the next processing step can execute the required custom logic. Based on this logic, the custom field YY1_DocumentPrint can be checked with value X to determine whether the document is eligible for further print processing.


3) Create a Utility Class in that class design solution like below and use in local event

3.1) first check SOLUTION ORDER items and collect all SALES ORDER, SERVICE ORDER and SERVICE CONTRACT using CDS view I_BusinessSolutionOrderItem.

3.2) For SALES ORDERS, use I_SalesOrder and I_SalesOrderItem to verify that the order is eligible for further processing. Check TotalCreditCheckStatus in I_SalesOrder to ensure that the Sales Order has successfully passed the credit check, and check ItemGeneralIncompletionStatus in I_SalesOrderItem to confirm that there are no incomplete mandatory item-level data entries. If both conditions are satisfied, proceed to the next step of the process; otherwise, exit the process.

3.3) Use I_ServiceDocumentItem to verify whether the Service Order Item is eligible for invoice order confirmation printing. Check the SrvcDocItemCreditStatus field to ensure that the required credit validation has been successfully completed. If the condition is satisfied, proceed to the next step; otherwise, exit the process and prevent the Solution Order from printing the Invoice Order Confirmation. Additional validation conditions may be incorporated based on customer-specific requirements and the availability of relevant data.3.4 If both conditions pass, use I_BusinessSolutionOrderTP and update Custom  yy1_documentprint.

For example:

        MODIFY ENTITIES OF i_businesssolutionordertp

                 ENTITY businesssolutionorder

                 UPDATE FIELDS ( yy1_documentprint_srh )

                 WITH VALUE #( (

                 businesssolutionorder = ls_salesOrder-BusinessSolutionOrder

                 yy1_documentprint_srh = abap_true

                 ) )

                 FAILED DATA(lt_failed_modify)

                 REPORTED DATA(lt_reported_modify).

This is to be done this way because, once item is released in Solution Order any field in the item including the custom fields cannot be modified. Hence, the custom field to control the output generation is created at solution order header

Configuring Output Parameter Determination

Solution order output availability will be controlled via the standard framework of Output Parameter Determination (OPD). To be specific, output will be blocked until the value of the custom field yy1_documentprint_srh is set to “X” via the technical implementation based on events described above. There are several steps required to set up OPD.

Firstly, it is needed to make the custom field “Document Print” available for usage in the Output Parameter Determination app:

Launch the Output Parameter Determination app and select the Business Solution Order object, for which we are going to enable the custom field yy1_documentprint_srh. Right-click on the dropdown box  Show Rules for and click on Refresh Condition Parameters of Application. Confirm data binding refresh and wait until it finishes.

 

Secondly, this custom field yy1_documentprint_srh need to be added to the Condition Table of the Output Relevance determination step. This will enable the check of the custom field value and make the output item relevant – and consequently allow printing out or sending an email:

Select Output Relevance in the Determination Step dropdown box. Then, click Edit and Table Settings. Click on Insert Column and then From Context Data Objects..

 

Search for the custom field Document Print, mark it with a checkbox and click OK.

 

As a result, the field will be added to the Condition Columns section. You can identify it by the prefix yy1, indicating that it is a custom field.

 

Now observe a new column added to the Table Contents. Click on the three dots in the Document Print column, enter the value “X” with an operator is equal to and click OK. Finally, click Activate to apply changes.

 

 

Test Case

Now let’s test how the development and output parameter determination will be blocking solution order confirmation output during the lifecycle.

Create a solution order entering in the Sold-To field a customer with credit check activated and credit exposure exceeding the limit in order to achieve credit check block in the follow-up documents.

 

Enter sales item, service item and service contract item without releasing them and save the solution order. This will result into the solution order with the status Open at header and items level. Consequently, since the status is still open, you can also observe that the custom field Document Print at the solution order header is empty, because it has not been affected by the custom logic we’ve developed.

 

Try printing out the solution order confirmation by selecting the output item and clicking Send Output. There will be an error that item is not relevant for output – because Document Print value is not equal to “X”, which is an Output Parameter Determination condition for Output Relevance.

 

As a next step, let’s release solution order items to trigger follow-up documents creation. Since the Sold-To of the solution order has the exceeded Credit Exposure, those documents will be blocked and the Solution Order Progress application will highlight them in red.

 

You can double check the reason for blocks in the documents to confirm that it happens due to credit check.

Service order:

 

Service contract:

 

For the sales order, let’s also cause document incompletion by deleting Incoterms data in the sales order header and Net/Gross Weight in the item:

 

Let’s remove credit check error in the follow-up documents by releasing them via the Manage Documented Credit Decisions application.

 

This will lead to the following state of the solution order progress: service order and service contract are no longer blocking the output, however, sales order is incomplete, therefore the custom field Document Print in the solution order header is still not impacted and order confirmation cannot be sent.

 

After entering Incoterms and Net/Gross Weight in the sales order header and item respectively, incompletion status of the sales order is removed, which is getting captured by our development and custom logic changes the value of the Document Print field to “X”.

 

Finally, Output Item can be sent or  printed successfully.

 

 

Summary

This blog demonstrates an approach to control solution order confirmation output based on certain conditions. Technically, this is achieved by:

  • Creation of a custom field at a solution order header level
  • Implementation of custom logic in a utility class, capturing events in solution order and follow-up documents and changing the value of the custom field (custom logic fulfil certain business conditions is written here to control the output)
  • Extension of solution order output parameter determination with the custom field

entry.title: Digital Signature – Signature Validation Error Using SSFW_KRN_SIGN and STRUST Certificate
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We are working on a Malaysia e-Invoice digital signature implementation in SAP ECC 6.0.

We have successfully imported our signing certificate into STRUST and are currently generating the digital signature using Function Module SSFW_KRN_SIGN.

However, when the signed XML is submitted to the portal, we receive a Signature Validation Error.

  • Is SSFW_KRN_SIGN the recommended function module for generating XML digital signatures in ECC 6.0, or should another FM/class be used?
  • For the signer information structure (SSFINFO), what values should be passed for:
    • ID (SSFID)
    • PROFILE (SSFPROF)
  • How can we verify that the certificate configured in STRUST is correctly being used during the signing process?

    Currently, the signature is being generated successfully, but the external portal reports a signature validation failure.

Any guidance or details would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

Despite this adjustment, the UI still not getting the associated value for DriverName and DriverMobile. It only updates after the saving the record.

Is there anything am I missing here ?
